category: Advanced Java / Spring Boot / Concurrency / JVM
description: Detailed answers for 32 advanced Java interview questions covering Spring Boot internals, concurrency patterns, JVM mechanisms, and production-grade design

answers.question: What is the exact startup flow of a Spring Boot application?
answers.answer: Spring Boot's startup is orchestrated by the SpringApplication class through a well-defined sequence of phases. First, the environment is prepared by gathering system properties, environment variables, and command-line arguments into a ConfigurableEnvironment. Second, an ApplicationContext is created — typically AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext for web apps. Third, classpath scanning discovers all @SpringBootApplication components including @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller classes. Fourth, @EnableAutoConfiguration triggers SpringFactoriesLoader to read META-INF/spring.factories files and load auto-configuration classes. Fifth, BeanPostProcessors are registered to enhance beans (AOP proxies, validation, etc.). Sixth, an embedded servlet container (Tomcat by default) is created and configured. Seventh, the context is refreshed, invoking all InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() and @PostConstruct methods. Finally, ApplicationReadyEvent is published, signaling the app is live. The entire flow is customizable via ApplicationContextInitializer and SpringApplicationRunListener interfaces, giving fine-grained control over each phase of startup.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot detect embedded Tomcat automatically?
answers.answer: Spring Boot detects an embedded Tomcat server automatically through a series of classpath presence checks powered by @ConditionalOnClass annotations. When the spring-boot-starter-web dependency is on the classpath, it brings in spring-webmvc and the Tomcat servlet container libraries. The ServletWebServerFactoryAutoConfiguration class is loaded by the auto-configuration mechanism and inspects the classpath for specific indicator classes such as javax.servlet.Servlet and org.apache.catalina.startup.Tomcat. If those classes are found, a TomcatServletWebServerFactory bean is registered as the server factory. If Jetty classes are detected instead (org.eclipse.jetty.server.Server), a JettyServletWebServerFactory is used. Undertow gets registered similarly. Spring uses @ConditionalOnMissingBean to ensure only one factory is created. The chosen factory is then invoked by ServletWebServerApplicationContext.createWebServer() during context refresh to spin up the embedded container. This entire detection-and-selection flow requires zero XML configuration — just the right JAR on the classpath triggers everything automatically through the convention-over-configuration philosophy.

answers.question: What happens if two beans of the same type exist without @Qualifier?
answers.answer: When multiple beans of the same type exist in the Spring ApplicationContext and no disambiguation hint is provided, Spring throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException at startup — not at runtime. This is a deliberate design choice: the container fails fast rather than silently injecting the wrong bean. The error message lists all matching bean names. There are four clean ways to resolve the ambiguity. First, annotate the injection point with @Qualifier("beanName") to pinpoint which implementation to use. Second, mark one implementation with @Primary so it becomes the default when no qualifier is specified. Third, name beans explicitly using @Bean("specificName") in a @Configuration class and match the qualifier to that name. Fourth, use constructor injection combined with @Qualifier for the clearest code — this also makes testing easier. In practice, @Primary is ideal when one implementation is the sensible default for 90% of use cases, while @Qualifier handles the exceptional cases. Relying on field injection with @Autowired alone is brittle; constructor injection with explicit qualifiers is preferred in production codebases.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot load profile-specific configurations?
answers.answer: Spring Boot profile-specific configuration loading is driven by the Environment abstraction. At startup, Spring first loads application.properties (or application.yml) as the base configuration. It then checks the spring.profiles.active property — set via command-line (--spring.profiles.active=prod), environment variable (SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE), or programmatically — and loads the matching application-{profile}.properties files. Profile-specific values override the base config for the same keys. Multiple profiles can be active simultaneously (e.g., spring.profiles.active=prod,metrics), and all their files are loaded in order. The spring.profiles.include property enables profile composition: activating "dev" could automatically pull in "logging" and "database-local" sub-profiles. The full property resolution priority order is: command-line args > OS environment variables > profile-specific files > base application files > defaults. The @Profile annotation on @Bean methods or @Configuration classes further restricts bean registration to specific active profiles. The Environment interface provides programmatic access: environment.getActiveProfiles() and environment.containsProperty("key").

answers.question: What is the role of SpringFactoriesLoader?
answers.answer: SpringFactoriesLoader is the discovery mechanism at the heart of Spring Boot's auto-configuration system. During startup it scans all JAR files on the classpath for META-INF/spring.factories files. Each file is a standard Java properties file where keys are fully-qualified interface or annotation names, and values are comma-separated lists of implementation class names. The most important key is org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.EnableAutoConfiguration, which maps to all auto-configuration classes a given library wants to contribute. Spring reads and merges every spring.factories it finds across all JARs, then evaluates each listed class against its @Conditional annotations to decide whether to include it. This design makes auto-configuration entirely modular: a third-party library (e.g., a Redis client) ships its own spring.factories pointing to its auto-configuration class, and Spring picks it up automatically without any manual @Import. In Spring Boot 2.7+ the mechanism was updated to use META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports files, but SpringFactoriesLoader remains the underlying infrastructure for all other factory types.

answers.question: How did Spring Boot remove XML configuration almost completely?
answers.answer: Spring Boot eliminated XML through @Configuration classes replacing bean definitions and @Bean annotations replacing <bean> elements. @SpringBootApplication meta-annotation combines @Configuration, @ComponentScan, @EnableAutoConfiguration enabling classpath scanning. Property files replaced property placeholders. @ConfigurationProperties replaced XML property configurations. Embedded application servers eliminated web.xml. Convention-over-configuration reduced boilerplate significantly while maintaining full configurability for edge cases.

answers.question: Difference between @RestController and @Controller internally?
answers.answer: @RestController internally combines @Controller + @ResponseBody at class level, automatically serializing return values to JSON. @Controller returns ModelAndView for template rendering. @RestController eliminates need for @ResponseBody on every method. Under hood, Spring's DispatcherServlet handles routing and response serialization differently for each. Both use same request mapping mechanism but differ in response handling philosophy.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot manage dependency versions automatically?
answers.answer: Spring Boot manages dependency versions through spring-boot-starter parent POM providing managed versions. Starters declare dependencies without versions, inheriting from parent. BOM (Bill of Materials) pattern defines compatible version sets. Maven/Gradle resolves exact versions from parent. When updating Spring Boot version, all compatible library versions update automatically. This prevents version conflict chaos and ensures library compatibility. Users can override specific versions if needed.

answers.question: What is the complete lifecycle of a Spring Bean?
answers.answer: Spring Bean lifecycle: 1) Constructor invoked 2) Properties/dependencies injected 3) *Aware interfaces called (BeanNameAware, ApplicationContextAware) 4) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization() 5) @PostConstruct method 6) InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() 7) init-method parameter 8) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() [AOP proxies applied] 9) Bean ready 10) @PreDestroy 11) DisposableBean.destroy() 12) destroy-method. Each hook customizable, enabling precise control over bean behavior.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot handle externalized configuration?
answers.answer: Spring Boot handles externalized configuration through Environment abstraction and PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer. Loads properties from application.properties/yml, environment variables, command-line args, and @ConfigurationProperties classes. PropertySourcesPropertyResolver resolves values when accessed. @Value annotation injects properties. Environment interface programmatically accesses all resolved properties. ConfigurableEnvironment allows adding PropertySources at runtime. This enables environment-specific configuration without code changes.

answers.question: What happens if application.yml and application.properties both exist?
answers.answer: If both application.yml and application.properties exist, Spring loads both but application.properties takes precedence (loaded second). However, YAML is recommended as standard. If both define same property, .properties value wins due to loading order. Best practice: use one format exclusively to avoid confusion. Spring Boot documentation recommends YAML for readability. Properties file loaded first, then YAML overrides it during initialization.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot integrate with Actuator internally?
answers.answer: Spring Boot Actuator auto-configuration creates actuator endpoints (health, metrics, env, beans) accessible via HTTP or JMX. ActuatorAutoConfiguration conditionally registers endpoint beans if spring-boot-actuator on classpath. EndpointSupplier discovers all Endpoint implementations. ManagementEndpointSupplier filters by management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property. WebEndpointSupplier creates HTTP endpoints for web exposure. JMX integration via @Endpoint annotation. Health indicators and metric collectors registered via HealthIndicator and MeterRegistry interfaces.

answers.question: How does @ConditionalOnClass work internally?
answers.answer: @ConditionalOnClass uses reflection to check if specified class exists on classpath during ApplicationContext initialization. Works with Java reflection to load Class object, catching ClassNotFoundException if missing. If class found, condition evaluates true and bean is registered. If class missing, condition false and bean skipped. Useful for optional features: register specific beans only if required libraries available. Evaluated during auto-configuration bean registration phase.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot enable AutoConfiguration automatically?
answers.answer: Spring Boot enables auto-configuration through @EnableAutoConfiguration meta-annotation processed by ConfigurationClassParser. Scans classpath for META-INF/spring.factories files via SpringFactoriesLoader.loadFactoryNames(). Loads all EnableAutoConfiguration classes listed there. Evaluates @Conditional annotations on each class determining inclusion. Uses AutoConfigurationSorter to order beans via @AutoConfigureBefore/@After. Creates auto-configuration beans which user @Configuration classes can override.

answers.question: What happens internally when you use @EnableAutoConfiguration?
answers.answer: @EnableAutoConfiguration processes automatically when @SpringBootApplication used (which includes it). SpringBootApplicationLoads spring.factories file via SpringFactoriesLoader. Creates AutoConfigurationImportSelector filtering classes by @Conditional evaluations. Calls selectImports() returning auto-configuration class names. Spring imports these as @Configuration classes. User-defined beans override auto-configured ones via @ConditionalOnMissingBean. Debug mode displays which configurations matched/unmatched.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot create embedded servlet containers?
answers.answer: Spring Boot creates embedded servlet containers via ServerBootstrapConfiguration detecting available servlet container JARs. TomcatServletWebServerFactory, JettyServletWebServerFactory, UndertowServletWebServerFactory registered based on classpath presence. WebServerFactoryCustomizer allows customization. ServletWebServerApplicationContext calls createWebServer() creating EmbeddedWebServer instance. Container configured with properties from application.properties (port, context-path, ssl). Container.start() binds to port and listens for requests.

answers.question: What happens internally when @ConfigurationProperties is used?
answers.answer: @ConfigurationProperties enables binding properties from application.yml to Java objects. PropertySource values mapped to class fields via Spring's TypeConverter supporting type coercion. Prefix property groups related values. @ConstructorBinding uses constructor injection. @EnableConfigurationProperties registers the class as Spring bean. Property name matching via camelCase conversion (my-property → myProperty). Validation via JSR-303 annotations. Nested properties supported via inner classes. Spring creates bean with all properties populated from config.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot implement dependency injection internally?
answers.answer: Spring Boot implements dependency injection via BeanFactory interface. AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor processes @Autowired annotations at bean instantiation. Searches ApplicationContext for matching beans by type, then name if multiple. Supports constructor injection, setter injection, field injection. Qualifier annotation specifies bean when multiple match. ObjectProvider provides lazy/optional injection. Resolver strategies use BeanDefinitionRegistry and ResolvableType for type matching. Circular dependency detection via early singleton exposure and ObjectFactory wrapping.

answers.question: What happens internally when @Autowired is processed?
answers.answer: @Autowired processed by AutowiredAnnotationBeanPostProcessor.postProcessProperties() during bean instantiation. Scans class for @Autowired annotations on fields, setters, constructors. Resolves each annotation via DependencyDescriptor containing type and name info. Uses BeanFactory.resolveDependency() finding matching bean. Applies TypeConverter for type coercion if needed. Handles Optional, ObjectProvider, List injection patterns. Calls setter/constructor or injects field. Validates required=true dependencies found, throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException if missing.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot resolve circular dependencies?
answers.answer: Spring resolves circular dependencies using early singleton exposure mechanism: 1) Bean A starts construction 2) A added to singletonFactories as ObjectFactory 3) A's dependencies (including B) are resolved 4) B added similarly 5) B's constructor needs A, getBean finds A in singletonFactories 6) ObjectFactory.getObject() returns partially initialized A 7) Circular reference resolved. Works for setter injection and field injection. Constructor injection circular references fail as instances needed before added to singletonFactories.

answers.question: What happens during Spring Boot application context initialization?
answers.answer: Application context initialization: 1) ApplicationContextInitializers called first 2) PropertySources prepared 3) BeanFactory created and configured 4) BeanFactoryPostProcessors applied (modify bean definitions) 5) PropertyPlaceholderConfigurer substitutes properties 6) Classpath scanning finds @Configuration, @Component classes 7) Beans registered in BeanDefinition registry 8) Remaining beans instantiated 9) BeanPostProcessors registered 10) Context refresh listeners notified 11) Singleton beans created 12) Application ready events published.

answers.question: What is the role of ApplicationContext vs BeanFactory?
answers.answer: ApplicationContext extends BeanFactory adding enterprise features: event publishing, message sourcing, environment support, resource loading, AOP integration. BeanFactory is low-level bean container, raw bean creation/wiring. ApplicationContext adds lifecycle support, configuration source resolution, property placeholder processing, internationalization. BeanFactory lazy-loads beans on request, ApplicationContext eagerly instantiates singletons. Use ApplicationContext for web/enterprise apps, BeanFactory for lightweight/embedded scenarios. ApplicationContext recommended for most applications due to comprehensive feature set.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot support multiple environments/profiles?
answers.answer: Spring profiles enable environment-specific configuration through spring.profiles.active property. @Profile annotation conditions beans on active profiles. application-{profile}.yml files provide profile-specific overrides. Multiple profiles activate simultaneously via comma-separated values. profiles.include allows profile composition (dev includes metrics). @ConditionalOnProfile provides runtime checks. Environment.getActiveProfiles() retrieves active profiles programmatically. PropertySource resolution prioritizes profile-specific properties. Eliminates need for conditional code or environment-specific builds.

answers.question: What happens internally when you use @SpringBootApplication?
answers.answer: @SpringBootApplication is a convenience meta-annotation that combines three foundational Spring annotations into one. First, @Configuration marks the class as a source of @Bean definitions, so Spring treats it as a configuration class. Second, @ComponentScan triggers classpath scanning starting from the annotated class's package and all its subpackages, discovering every @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller class automatically — no manual bean registration needed. Third, @EnableAutoConfiguration instructs Spring to read META-INF/spring.factories (or the newer AutoConfiguration.imports file) and load all qualifying auto-configuration classes based on what libraries are on the classpath. The three annotations are equivalent to writing them explicitly: using @SpringBootApplication is simply the idiomatic convention. It is almost always placed on the main class alongside the main() method. Auto-configuration classes implement @Conditional checks so they only activate when their requirements are satisfied, giving users a working application with zero boilerplate while remaining fully overridable by defining their own beans.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot enable hot reload using DevTools?
answers.answer: Spring DevTools enables hot reload through embedded LiveReload server and classpath resource watching. On classpath change, RestartClassLoader reloads application context without full JVM restart. Faster than full restart. DevToolsPropertyDefaultsFactory adds development property defaults (caching disabled, debug logging enabled). RemoteSpringBootApplication enables remote restart for deployed apps. LiveReload browser extension automatically refreshes page. Excluded resources from restart (images, CSS). Disables caching by default. Development-only feature disabled in production.

answers.question: How does HashMap work internally? Explain buckets and collision handling.
answers.answer: HashMap uses bucket array storing key-value pairs. hashCode() determines bucket index via index = hashCode() % array.length. Collisions (multiple keys → same bucket) handled with linked lists initially, converting to red-black trees if bucket size exceeds 8 (Java 8+). get() typically O(1) average case, O(log n) if tree. Resizing occurs at 75% load factor, doubling capacity and rehashing entries. Not thread-safe, allows null key/values. Performance depends on hash function quality and load factor tuning.

answers.question: How does ConcurrentHashMap handle concurrency at the segment level?
answers.answer: ConcurrentHashMap uses segment-based locking allowing concurrent writes to different segments. Java 8+ uses node-level CAS (Compare-And-Swap) instead of segment locks. get() mostly lock-free via volatile visibility. Multiple threads write simultaneously to different segments without blocking. putIfAbsent, compute provide atomic operations. forEach supports parallelism. Read operations don't require locking. Collision handling identical to HashMap. Resize only affects affected segment. Highly suitable for multi-threaded environments with expected contention.

answers.question: Explain Thread Pools and Executor Framework with sizing strategies.
answers.answer: Thread pools execute tasks using reusable threads reducing creation overhead. CPU-bound tasks: size = Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors(). IO-bound tasks: size = processors × (1 + waitTime/computeTime). Fixed pools maintain constant threads. Cached pools create on demand. Work-stealing (ForkJoinPool) optimal for divide-conquer. Bounded queues prevent memory issues. Rejection policies (Abort, Discard, CallerRuns) handle overflow. Core/max size configuration critical. Graceful shutdown via shutdown() then awaitTermination() essential for cleanup.

answers.question: What causes Deadlock and what prevention mechanisms exist?
answers.answer: Deadlock occurs when threads form circular wait: Thread A holds Lock1 waiting Lock2, Thread B holds Lock2 waiting Lock1. Four necessary conditions: mutual exclusion (non-shareable resources), hold-and-wait (hold while requesting), no preemption (can't forcibly take), circular wait. Prevention: 1) Lock ordering (same acquisition order) 2) Timeouts (tryLock(timeout)) 3) Atomic operations avoiding locks 4) Reduce hold-and-wait period. Detection via ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads(). Avoid nested lock acquisitions.

answers.question: Explain Java GC algorithms (G1, CMS, ZGC) and trade-offs.
answers.answer: Serial GC single-threaded, high pause times, minimal overhead. Parallel GC multi-threaded, high throughput, longer pauses. CMS concurrent collection, low pause times, high CPU usage. G1 region-based, balanced approach, suitable large heaps. ZGC sub-millisecond pauses via concurrent collection. Generational assumption: young objects die young. Young generation collected frequently. Old generation rarely. Trade-offs: Serial minimizes memory, Parallel maximizes throughput, CMS/G1/ZGC minimize pause. Choose based on latency/throughput requirements.

answers.question: How does Spring Boot Auto Configuration work and resolution order?
answers.answer: Auto-configuration loads via SpringFactoriesLoader discovering EnableAutoConfiguration classes from spring.factories. Evaluates @Conditional annotations determining inclusion (ConditionalOnClass, ConditionalOnMissingBean, ConditionalOnProperty). Orders via @AutoConfigureBefore/@After controlling load sequence. User @Configuration beans override auto-configured ones via @ConditionalOnMissingBean. Debug mode shows matched/unmatched conditions. spring.autoconfigure.exclude property excludes specific auto-configs. Convention-over-configuration provides sensible defaults overridable by users.

answers.question: Explain Bean Lifecycle in Spring with all hooks.
answers.answer: Bean lifecycle: 1) Constructor instantiation 2) Dependency injection 3) *Aware interfaces (BeanNameAware, ApplicationContextAware) 4) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization() 5) @PostConstruct method 6) InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() 7) init-method 8) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() [AOP] 9) Ready for use 10) @PreDestroy 11) DisposableBean.destroy() 12) destroy-method. Customizable hooks enable initialization validation, resource setup, AOP enhancement, graceful shutdown.
